coredata.dc:description: As one of the most important cathode materials for aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs), polyaniline (PANI) has attracted much attention because of its high electrical conductivity, simple synthesis procedure and excellent redox reversibility. However, the morphology of PANI macromolecules and its spontaneous deprotonation properties lead to low capacity and poor cycle stability, which limits its further application. To address these issues, in this work, PANI is in-situ prepared on the surface of carbon cloth (CC) through chemical oxidation polymerization under the presence of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). Compared with pure PANI cathode electrodes, the materials with appropriate amount of CPDs (CPDs/PANI@CC-10) show a high specific discharge capacity of 168 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1. Even at a high current density of 10 A g−1, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 still exhibits a capacity of 102 mAh g−1, and maintains 94.3 % of the capacity after 10,000 cycles. The profound mechanism investigation reveals that during the charge/discharge cycles, the percentage of protonated N greatly increases due to the addition of CPDs, and new NZnO bonds are formed, which is beneficial to more stable cycle and larger amount of Zn2+ storage. This work provides a new route for control the morphology and structure of PANI, which is of significant for its practical application in AZIBs.

head.ce:title: Regulation of NZnO bonds via carbonized polymer dots in polyaniline composite cathode for high performance stable aqueous Zn-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sustained and Advanced Functional Materials, College of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sustained and Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Sustained and Advanced Functional Materials, College of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the most important cathode materials for aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs), polyaniline (PANI) has attracted much attention because of its high electrical conductivity, simple synthesis procedure and excellent redox reversibility. However, the morphology of PANI macromolecules and its spontaneous deprotonation properties lead to low capacity and poor cycle stability, which limits its further application. To address these issues, in this work, PANI is in-situ prepared on the surface of carbon cloth (CC) through chemical oxidation polymerization under the presence of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). Compared with pure PANI cathode electrodes, the materials with appropriate amount of CPDs (CPDs/PANI@CC-10) show a high specific discharge capacity of 168 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1. Even at a high current density of 10 A g−1, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 still exhibits a capacity of 102 mAh g−1, and maintains 94.3 % of the capacity after 10,000 cycles. The profound mechanism investigation reveals that during the charge/discharge cycles, the percentage of protonated N greatly increases due to the addition of CPDs, and new NZnO bonds are formed, which is beneficial to more stable cycle and larger amount of Zn2+ storage. This work provides a new route for control the morphology and structure of PANI, which is of significant for its practical application in AZIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) have attracted much attention because of their safety, non-toxicity, low cost, high theoretical capacity (820 mAh g−1) and low redox potential (−0.76 V versus standard hydrogen electrode), etc [1,2]. However, the higher valence and larger ionic radius of Zn2+ lead to the slow ion dynamics and collapse or damage of the cathode material structure during charge and discharge, which is manifested as cycle instability [3–6]. Therefore, one of the most important issues for AZIBs is to develop cathode materials with sufficient capacity and good cycle stability [7,8].

At present, cathode materials for AZIBs can be mainly divided into four categories, namely Mn-based materials [9–11], V-based materials [12–14], Prussian blue analogues [15–17], conductive polymers [18,19] and conversion-typed materials [20]. Compared with metal cathode materials, conductive polymer is one of the most promising materials due to its advantages of regeneration, stable structure and good flexibility [21,22]. As one of the most typical conductive polymers, polyaniline (PANI) is regarded as an attractive cathode material for AZIBs because of its good electrical conductivity, simple synthesis method and excellent redox reversibility [23,24]. However, PANI still has some shortcomings that prevent its further application. Existed as macromolecular state at most cases for PANI, some ions such as SO4 2− and CF3SO3 − cannot enter the interior of PANI macromolecules [25]. As a result, PANI cannot be fully utilized and hence the specific capacity of PANI based zinc-batteries is only 100–200 mAh g−1. In addition, the spontaneous deprotonation of PANI in weakly acidic electrolyte such as ZnSO4 and Zn(CF3SO3)2, leads to the inactivation of PANI, which results in poor cycle stability and rate performance [26–28]. For example, the material obtained by copolymerization of aniline and aniline-2, 5-disulfonic acid through an electrochemical oxidative method can only cycle 1000 times at 5 A g−1 in Zn(CF3SO3)2 electrolyte [29].

Adjusting micro-morphology by self-assembly and in-situ polymerization strategies is an effective approach to improve the utilization rate of PANI [30]. For example, Wu et al. [31] successfully synthesized cathode materials with well-arranged fibrous morphology by self-assembling PANI and sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate. The resulted nanoribbons composed of well-arranged nanofibers enabled the battery to exhibit higher specific capacity (95 mAh g−1) and better rate performance. Even so, the polyaniline involved in the electrochemical reaction is still mostly concentrated on the surface of the materials, leading to the limited improvement of capacity as well as its cycle stability.

In order to solve the problem of spontaneous deprotonation, doping or composite strategies are used to modify the PANI molecules [32–34]. For example, copolymerization of aniline and metanilic acid was conducted, in which the equivalent of self-doped-SO3 − was used as a proton reservoir for PANI skeleton, leading to its good electrochemical properties in ZnSO4 electrolyte [35]. In addition, Gu et al. [36] prepared N-doped carbon substrate by pyrolysis of ZIF-8 and then growth of PANI. The abundant pyrrorole-N functional groups on the carbon skeleton provided a local proton environment for PANI, making it possible to achieve relatively high capacity and stable cycle performance (125 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1 and retain 93 % of the capacity over 5000 cycles). However, the control of protonation for PANI still remains a great challenge, and especially, the corresponding mechanism of electrochemical performance improvement resulting from protonation reaction is unrevealed.

In this work, the issues mentioned above were addressed by introduction of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with a special core–shell structure [37,38]. The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in the shell structure of CPDs were used to modify PANI molecules. On one hand, CPDs regulate PANI nanocones to grow more densely and uniformly on carbon cloth (CC), which greatly improved the utilization rate of active PANI, and show a high specific discharge capacity of 168 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1. On the other hand, CPDs provide protons to PANI, making Zn-PANI batteries show more stable cycle performance and better rate performance. Even under the high current density of 10 A g−1, it can still maintain the specific capacity of 102 mAh g−1 with the capacity retention rate as high as 94.3 % after 10,000 cycles. Moreover, the existence of NZnO bond during the cycles is proved through electrochemical tests and characterization analysis, which is conducive to improving the capacity of the batteries and cycle stability.

Citric acid (99.5 %) and aniline (99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (98 %) and ammonium persulfate (98 %) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents utilized in this work were analytical grade and not further purified before use.

CPDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors [39]. Firstly, 6.3 g citric acid was dissolved in 60 mL deionized water and vigorously stirred for 10 min. Then, 2 mL ethylenediamine was added to this solution and continued to stir for 20 min. This solution was transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After that, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and dialyzed for 24 h to remove unreacted substances or other impurities. Specifically, after the reaction was cooled to room temperature, the macromolecules were removed using a 13 mm aqueous polyethersulfone (PES) filter membrane, and then the filtrate was transferred to a dialysis bag (MD44-500) heated in boiling water using a 5 mL syringe. The dialysis bag was sealed at both ends and placed in deionized water, and the water was changed every 12 h. After 24 h of dialysis, the color of the distilled water became significantly lighter, indicating that small molecular impurities or unreacted materials were removed. Finally, the product was freeze-dried to obtain a yellow powder solid.

PANI was grown on the surface of CC by a simple chemical oxidation polymerization method. Firstly, 183 μL aniline and some content of CPDs were added to 100 mL dilute hydrochloric acid (1 M). In the meanwhile, 0.31 g ammonium persulfate was added to another 100 mL dilute hydrochloric acid (1 M). After stirring 1 h, a piece of acid-washed CC (5*5 cm2) was put into the dilute aniline solution, and then both of solutions were placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Then, the two solutions were evenly mixed and continued to be placed in the refrigerator for 24 h to grow the PANI on the surface of the CC. After that, the sample was removed and continued to immerse into deionized water for 24 h to remove doping. Finally, the sample was dried at 80 °C and labeled as CPDs/PANI@CC-X (X = 5, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50) for the addition of CPDs 5, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mg, respectively. The synthesis procedure of PANI@CC was basically the same as that of CPDs/PANI@CC-X, except that no CPDs were added, which was named PANI@CC.

The morphology of CPDs was obtained by Field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-2010F). X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Smartlab) was used to characterize the structure of CPDs, PANI@CC and CPDs/PANI@CC-X at a rate of 10° or 20° per min. Fluorescence emission spectrum was performed with Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NICOLET iS50) and Raman spectroscopy (Raman, JY HR-800 Lab Ram) were used to characterize the functional groups on the surface of the material. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI SU8010) was used to observe the microstructure of the materials. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, JEOL-2100F) was used to observe the distribution of elements. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, FV3000) was used to observe the fluorescence properties of samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFischer, ESCALAB 250Xi) employing Al Kα excitation was used to analyze the type, content and bonding of elements in the samples.

PANI@CC and CPDs/PANI@CC-X as cathodes were used to assemble CR2032 coin battery. The anode zinc foil was polished, and 100 μL of 2 M Zn(CF3SO3)2 (Aladdin) solution was used as the electrolyte. The separator was glass fiber membrane (Whatman). The cycle performance of all electrode materials was performed between 0.4 V and 1.4 V on a NEWARE CT4008T test system. Electrochemical impedance testing (EIS) was performed on an electrochemical workstation (chi 660E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed on the other electrochemical workstation (chi 750E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument). Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique (GITT) was performed on a LAND CT2001A test system. During the GITT test, after 5 cycles to activate the cell, the current (0.1 A g−1) lasted for 10 min and then relaxed for 30 min, which was repeated during cycle processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Control the interaction between CPDs and PANI
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis route of cathode material CPDs/PANI@CC-X is shown in Scheme 1 . PANI with nanocone morphology is in-situ prepared on the surface of CC through chemical oxidation polymerization under the presence of CPDs. During the polymerization, CPDs provide protons by formation of interaction and simultaneously control the morphology of PANI in order to improve the utilization of cathode.

From Fig. 1 a, XRD spectrum of the pure CC shows strong peaks at 25° and 43° corresponding to the (002) and (100/101) lattice plane of graphitic carbon, respectively. After modified by CPDs and PANI, the peaks resulted from PANI can be observed in XRD difference spectra of PANI@CC and CPDs/PANI@CC-X (Fig. S1), indicating that PANI successfully grows on CC surface [40]. Moreover, compared with pure PANI, the peaks in CPDs/PANI@CC-X show significant peak shift, which become more obvious with increasing the contents of CPDs from 10 to 50 mg CPDs, indicating that there exists interaction between CPDs and PANI.

This interaction between CPDs and PANI is further determined by FTIR spectra. From Fig. 1b, the strong peak at 3400 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of PANI@CC results from N-H. In addition, peaks at 1496, 1456, 1290 and 1242 cm−1 are ascribed to CC quinoid, CC benzenoid, CN benzenoid, and CN quinoid groups, respectively [41,42]. However, the absence of significant peaks at both 1290 and 1242 cm−1 in CPDs/PANI@CC-10 suggests that abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on CPDs may have interaction with CN and CN (Fig. S2) [43]. When the CPDs content increases to 50 mg, the peak at 3400 cm−1 becomes significantly wider, and a new peak appears at 3200 cm−1. In addition, new peaks appear at 1556 and 1007 cm−1, corresponding to CO and epoxides in CPDs, respectively. This is because too much CPDs composited with PANI, resulting in the detection of peaks from CPDs.

The interaction between CPDs and PANI is further investigated by Raman spectra. From Fig. 1c, PANI@CC shows a typical PANI peaks at 1162, 1222, 1330, 1472 and 1593 cm−1, corresponding to imines, CH, CN+, CN quinoid, and CC benzenoid, respectively [44,45]. For CPDs/PANI@CC-10 and CPDs/PANI@CC-50, the peak corresponding to CN quinoid at 1472 cm−1 moves to 1460 cm−1, implying that there exists interaction between CPDs and CN quinoid. This interaction is favor for the improvement of protonation degree for PANI, which is further confirmed by XPS.

From Fig. 1d–f, the main elements in the XPS full spectrum of PANI@CC (Fig. S3a) and CPDs/PANI@CC-10 are C, N, and O. The O element of PANI@CC probably originates from the surface of the acid-washed CC or adsorption to air. As shown in the C 1s spectrum of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 (Fig. 1e), the peaks at 284.4, 285.2, 285.8, and 287.8 eV correspond to CC (CC), CN (CO, CN), CO (CN+), and OCO, respectively [46,47]. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f), the four peaks of 399.0, 399.7, 400.8 and 402.5 eV correspond to −N=, –NH-, –NH+=, and –NH+-, respectively [48,49]. The protonated N content in CPDs/PANI@CC-10 is calculated to be ∼37.7 %. In contrast, the corresponding peak of –NH+- at 402.5 eV disappears and hence there are only three peaks in the N 1s spectrum of PANI@CC (Fig. S3b). The protonated N content in PANI@CC is reduced to ∼19.1 %. Therefore, CPDs interact with CN on the long chain of PANI and provide protons to it, which significantly increases the protonation degree of N in CPDs/PANI@CC-10 composites. It is worth noted that for PANI material, the degree of protonation determines the cycle stability and rate performance. Therefore, materials with a high degree of protonation will have better electrochemical properties.

The introduction of CPDs is also important for the control of PANI morphology, which is a key factor for the improvement of capacity by enhancing the utilities of PANI. CLSM was used to confirm the presence of CPDs on CPDs/PANI@CC-10. From Fig. S4, an obvious blue fluorescence signal resulted from CPDs is observed on the surface of CPDs/PANI@CC-10. This fluorescence signal indicates the uniform growth of CPDs/PANI composites on the CC. In addition, the TEM image of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 shows that CPDs with clear edges are evenly distributed in the resulted composite electrodes (Fig. S5).

The morphology of PANI can be easily tuned by changing CPDs content. SEM images of CC show that the surface is smooth and uniform (Fig. S6). The growth of PANI on PANI@CC and CPDs/PANI@CC-10 is uniform, while that on CPDs/PANI@CC-50 is chaotic (Fig. 1g–i). Insets of Fig. 1g and h, the morphologies of PANI in PANI@CC and CPDs/PANI@CC-10 are nanocones, and the PANI nanocones on CPDs/PANI@CC-10 are much denser than those on PANI@CC. This is because CPDs involve in the formation of PANI nanocones and provide more active sites for the growth of PANI. This is conducive to increasing the contact area between PANI and the electrolyte, thereby increasing the utilization of PANI and resulting in a higher capacity. For CPDs/PANI@CC-50, the morphology of PANI changes from nanocones to nanoblocks, indicating that excessive local polymerization of aniline occurs at high content CPDs (Fig. 1i). This may lead to decrease in the contact area between the electrolyte and CPDs/PANI@CC-50, which further leads to the low utilization rate of PANI and the difficulty of Zn2+ insertion and extraction. Therefore, the optimized content of CPDs is 10 mg. In addition, Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 show a uniform distribution of elements C, N, O on the surface of CC (Fig. 1j–l).

The cycle performance of PANI@CC, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 and CPDs/PANI@CC-50 was tested at 0.1 A g−1 and 1 A g−1, respectively. From Fig. 2 a and b, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 exhibits a high discharge specific capacity of 168 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, which is much higher than that of PANI@CC (137 mAh g−1) and CPDs/PANI@CC-50 (133 mAh g−1). Similarly, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 at 1 A g−1 shows discharge specific capacity of 136 mAh g−1, which is also higher than that of PANI@CC (117 mAh g−1) and CPDs/PANI@CC-50 (113 mAh g−1). The capacity contributed (2.7 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1) by CC can be ignored (Fig. S7). Therefore, the capacity displayed by the materials is derived from the PANI or CPDs/PANI composites growing on the surface of CC. The increase in capacity at the early stage of the cycle is because the large specific surface area provided by the PANI nanocone makes it difficult for electrolyte fully in contact with PANI. With the continuous cycles, the electrolyte gradually permeates and more PANI is utilized, so it shows gradually increase in capacity. When the PANI is in full contact with the electrolyte, the batteries exhibit the highest capacity.

In order to explore the effects of different CPDs content on the electrochemical properties of CPDs/PANI@CC-X, the cycle performance of CPDs/PANI@CC-5, CPDs/PANI@CC-20, CPDs/PANI@CC-30, and CPDs/PANI@CC-40 were also tested (Fig. S8). CPDs/PANI@CC-10 also has the highest discharge specific capacity and the best cycle stability. This further confirms the conclusion that the density of PANI is the best value to ensure the full presence of its electrochemical performance at the CPDs content of 10 mg.

The rate performance of PANI@CC, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 and CPDs/PANI@CC-50 were further compared (Fig. 2c). When the current density is 0.5, 1, 2, 5 and 10 A g−1, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 exhibits high discharge specific capacity of 123, 118, 112, 106 and 102 mAh g−1. When the current is back to 0.5 A g−1, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 can still show the specific capacity of 117 mAh g−1, and its capacity retention rate is 95.12 %. PANI@CC shows the capacity of 113, 110, 106, 100 and 95 mAh g−1 at different current densities. In addition, CPDs/PANI@CC-50 shows the specific capacity of 110, 107, 103, 99 and 94 mAh g−1. They are all lower than those of CPDs/PANI@CC-10, indicating that CPDs/PANI@CC-10 has higher discharge specific capacity and better rate performance.

In order to further investigate the electrochemical behavior of the materials, PANI@CC, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 and CPDs/PANI@CC-50 were studied by EIS (Fig. 2d). Their impedance curves are composed of semicircles in the high frequency region and straight lines in the low frequency region. The equivalent circuit used to fit the EIS data of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 is shown in Fig. S9a, which include the Ohmic impedance (Rs), charge-transfer impedance (Rct), Warburg impedance (Wo), and constant phase angle element (CPE) [23]. CPDs/PANI@CC-10 exhibits the smallest charge transfer impedance (129 Ω), which indicates more intimate contact between CPDs/PANI@CC-10 and the electrolyte. It is worth noted that the charge transfer impedance of CPDs/PANI@CC-50 (169 Ω) is smaller than that of PANI@CC (246 Ω), which may be due to the excessively dispersed growth of PANI nanoblocks on the surface of CPDs/PANI@CC-50. In addition, the batteries after 100 cycles at 0.1 A g−1 were also studied by EIS (Fig. S9b). The charge transfer impedance (462 Ω) of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 is still smaller than that of the other two (532 Ω and 505 Ω, respectively).

The batteries after 100 cycles at 0.1 A g−1 were disassembled and their cathode materials were tested by SEM (Fig. S10). CPDs/PANI@CC-10 still maintains a well-defined morphology, implying that the stability of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 during the cycles is better. In addition, to determine whether CPDs shed during the cycles, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 was tested by CLSM (Fig. S11). Clear blue fluorescence is seen through the dark field and the compound field, indicating that CPDs don’t fall off during the cycles. The cycle performance of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 was also tested at high current density. CPDs/PANI@CC-10 shows a high discharge specific capacity of 102 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1 and 96 mAh g−1 after 10,000 cycles (Fig. 2f). Its capacity retention rate is as high as 94.3 % and the coulomb efficiency is about 99.9 %. After 10,000 cycles, the anode and cathode remain integrity (Fig. S12a). The surface of Zn anode is still relatively flat, no obvious dendrites are observed (Fig. S12b), and there are still obvious nanostructures on the surface of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 (Fig. S12c and d). It indicates that both anode and cathode show relatively good stability during the cycle of 10,000 cycles. However, PANI@CC can only run 3774 cycles at 10 A g−1 and only has a capacity of 68 mAh g−1 (Fig. S13). CPDs/PANI@CC-50 shows discharge specific capacity of 91 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1 and the capacity retention rate is 88.7 % after 10,000 cycles. Compared with PANI materials mentioned in previous reports, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 exhibits significantly higher discharge specific capacity and more stable cycle performance (Table S1). It confirms that the interaction between CPDs and PANI makes the batteries show better electrochemical performance. Finally, two coin batteries were connected in series and a small red bulb was successfully lit, which shows that the material has a certain application potential (Fig. 2e).

In the charge/discharge curve of PANI@CC, there are two charging platforms at 1.20 V and 1.03 V, and two discharge platforms at 1.11 V and 0.86 V (Fig. 3 a). Similarly, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 has two charging platforms at 1.21 V and 1.05 V, and two discharge platforms at 1.15 V and 0.85 V (Fig. 3b). With increasing current density, the platforms become less obvious but still exist. CPDs/PANI@CC-50 also has two pairs of similar charge/discharge platforms (Fig. S14a). It indicates the addition of CPDs doesn’t have much effect on the charging and discharging platforms of PANI, but adding appropriate amount of CPDs can make the material have better rate performance.

The reaction kinetics of the batteries were further tested by GITT test. Fig. S14b-d shows the corresponding ΔEt and ΔEs, charge/discharge curve and time–voltage curve of CPDs/PANI@CC-10. The ion diffusion coefficient (DZn 2+) can be calculated via eq (1) [50,51].

where D is the ion diffusion coefficient, τ is the relaxation time (s), nm is the number of molar, Vm is the molar volume, S is the contact area, ΔEs is the voltage change during charging or discharging and ΔEt is the voltage change when it is resting to the equilibrium state. CPDs/PANI@CC-10 has higher DZn 2+ during both charge and discharge processes (Fig. 3c). When charged, the DZn 2+ of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 is 2.85 × 10−11 cm2 s−1, while in the discharge state it is 3.1 × 10−11 cm2 s−1. In contrast, the DZn 2+ of PANI@CC is only 1.82 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 when charging and 1.88 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 when discharging. This implies that the addition of appropriate amount of CPDs makes ion transport more rapid, and thus enables the battery to have a faster reaction dynamic process and better rate performance.

CV was also used to evaluate the dynamics of batteries. Both of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 and PANI@CC have two obvious oxidation peaks at approximately 1.20 V and 1.04 V, while there are two reduction peaks at 1.07 V and 0.78 V (Fig. 3d and g). Further, lgi and lg ν are linearly fitted via eq (2) and eq (3) to get the slope b (Fig. 3e and h) [52,53].
(2)
i = a
ν
b
(3)
l g i = b l g ν + l g a
where i represents the peak current and ν represents different scan rates. When its slope b = 0.5, it means that the current is controlled by the diffusion behavior, and when b = 1, it means that the current is mainly controlled by the pseudocapacitance. It is calculated that b values of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 are 0.96, 1.02, 0.88 and 0.92 at different peaks, which is better than that of PANI@CC (0.96, 0.86, 0.79, 0.92).

In addition, the contribution ratios of pseudocapacitance and diffusion are calculated according to eq (4) and eq (5) [54,55].
(4)

k 1

(5)

where
k 1

represents the part contributed by the pseudocapacitance and

represents the part controlled by the diffusion. Under different sweep speeds, the pseudocapacitance contribution rate of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 is 67 %, 69 %, 75 %, 83 % and 88 % (Fig. 3f), which is much higher than those of PANI@CC (42 %, 45 %, 52 %, 64 % and 66 %) (Fig. 3i) and CPDs/PANI@CC-50 (63 %, 65 %, 69 %, 76 % and 83 %) (Fig. S15). In general, the pseudocapacitance reaction is faster, so CPDs/PANI@CC-10 has a faster reaction kinetics process and hence show better rate performance.

In order to determine the energy storage mechanism of the interaction in the cycle process, a series of characterizations are used to analyze CPDs/PANI@CC-10 cathode during different charge and discharge processes. First of all, ex-situ XRD was used to detect whether a new phase was created during the cycle. Fig. 4 a indicates the charge–discharge profile of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 in the voltage range 0.4 V-1.4 V at 0.1 A g−1 and Fig. 4b is corresponding ex-site XRD spectrum. Comparing these spectra to each other, no new diffraction peaks are generated. However, there is a weak but significant displacement at the scan angle of 25°–27°, as shown in the selected amplified ex-site XRD spectra (Fig. 4c). In addition, comparing the XRD patterns of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 before and after the cycle, no new phase is generated (Fig. S16). These results show that no new phase is fabricated during the charge–discharge cycle, but the insertion of Zn2+ into the cathode causes a change in the material structure, revealing that Zn2+ has a tendency to insert inner CPDs/PANI@CC-10 to form new chemical bond, rather than producing a new zinc salts.

Ex-situ XPS was used to further analyze the variation of Zn2+ forms during charge and discharge process. There are six elements Zn, F, O, N, C and S in the full spectrums of XPS, in which F and S elements come from the Zn(CF3SO3)2 electrolyte (Fig. 4d). By comparing the peak strength of Zn 2p, the Zn 2p peak gradually increases with the discharge, and gradually decreases with the charging, which indicates that the material has relatively good cycle reversibility. However, by comparing the Zn 2p peaks of PANI@CC and CPDs/PANI@CC-10 at the original state and discharge to 0.4 V, it can be found that the Zn 2p peak strength of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 becomes significantly stronger and exhibits significant peak shift (Fig. 4e). This indicates that more Zn2+ is stored by the cathode during the cycle, and Zn2+ is present in the electrode material with a new chemical state.

In order to determine how Zn2+ is stored by CPDs/PANI@CC-10 during the cycle, the Zn 2p peaks of PANI@CC and CPDs/PANI@CC-10 were further analyzed. The full spectrum of XPS of PANI@CC after cycle shows that the existing elements are the same as CPDs/PANI@CC-10, which are Zn, F, O, N, C, S (Fig. S17a). When discharged to 0.4 V, there are two Zn 2p peaks of the PANI@CC, and each of which can be divided into only one fitting peak (Fig. S17b). This shows that Zn2+ exists in only one chemical state, which can be attributed to the interaction between Zn2+ and the negatively charged functional groups on the long chain of PANI [27].

Similarly, Zn 2p spectra of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 discharged to different voltages are analyzed. There are two Zn 2p peaks of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 when discharged to 0.95 V or 0.4 V. In contrast, when discharged to 0.95 V, each Zn 2p peak can only be divided into one fitting peak (Fig. 4f). This indicates that only one form of Zn2+ is present in the material when discharged to 0.95 V. Considering the fitting peak result of PANI@CC (Fig. S16b), when discharged to 0.95 V, Zn2+ mainly interacts with the long chain of PANI through electrostatic force, and does not enter the interior of the composite. However, when discharged to 0.4 V, each Zn 2p peak can be divided into two fitted peaks, indicating the presence of two forms of Zn2+ (Fig. 4g). Combined with the ex-situ XRD test, the two existing forms of Zn2+, one is adsorbed on the electrode surface, and the other is Zn2+ enters the composite to form a new chemical bond, so the Zn 2p peak has a significant shift (Fig. 4e).

In order to determine the type of chemical bond formed by the insertion of Zn2+ into the interior of the composite, the N 1s and O 1s peaks of the material at 0.4 V were analyzed. Compared with the four fitting peaks in the initial state, the N 1s XPS spectrum after the cycle shows only three fitting peaks at 400.1 eV, 399.5 eV, and 398.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 4h). They correspond to –NH-, −N-Zn- and −N−-, respectively [42,46]. In addition, in O 1s XPS spectrum after the cycle, three fitting peaks are shown at 533.4 eV, 532.0 eV and 531.1 eV, corresponding to CO, CO and ZnO, respectively (Fig. 4i) [56,57]. However, the O 1s spectrum of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 before cycle shows three fitting peaks at 534.5 eV, 533.1 eV and 531.6 eV, corresponding to OH, CO and CO respectively (Fig. S18). It indicates that Zn2+ is inserted into the composite and interacts with the oxygen-containing functional group (OH). The resulting ZnO bond replaces the original OH bond, thus enabling further storage of Zn2+. Therefore, the presence of a special structure NZnO bond during the cycle was demonstrated by ex-situ XPS. Through the above electrochemical test and characterization analysis, the storage mechanism of Zn2+ in Zn-CPDs/PANI@CC-10 aqueous battery can be explained clearly (Fig. 5 ). During the discharge, Zn2+ migrates from the anode electrode to the cathode electrode. In the first part, Zn2+ interacts with the long chain of PANI by electrostatic force and is adsorbed on the surface of PANI. H+ enters the composite and participates in the reaction according to the traditional H+ insertion mechanism [27]. Specifically, the oxidation state, half-oxidation state and reduction state can be transformed into each other in the process of charge and discharge. In the second part, with the continuous discharge, Zn2+ enters the interior of PANI macromolecules and reacts with PANI long chains and oxygen-containing functional group of CPDs to produce new chemical bond NZnO, so as to achieve more storage of Zn2+ and faster reaction kinetics. At the same time, since the NZnO bond is more stable than the electrostatic force, it can also effectively improve the cycle stability. The reaction mechanism during charging is opposite to that during discharging. With increasing voltage, Zn2+ departs from the NZnO bond and moves towards the anode. CPDs provide protons to PANI through abundant oxygen-containing functional groups to improve the protonation degree of CPDs/PANI@CC-10. Therefore, PANI maintain a high activity and achieve a long-term cycle. The processes of Zn2+ insertion and de-insertion do not lead to the collapse or damage of the composite structure, which is also very important to improve the cycle stability of the battery.

In summary, the CPDs/PANI composite was grown in situ on the surface of the CC by a simple chemical oxidation polymerization method and used as cathode material for AZIBs. The characterization of morphology and structure reveals that the addition of appropriate CPDs can promote the growth of PANI nanocones to be moredense and uniform, and improve the utilization rate of PANI. CPDs can provide protons for CN in the long chain of PANI, which significantly improves the protonation degree of PANI. A series of electrochemical tests show that CPDs/PANI@CC-10 has great electrochemical performance. The addition of CPDs not only makes the battery show higher capacity, but also greatly improves the cycle stability and rate performance. When the current density is 0.1 A g−1, the discharge specific capacity of CPDs/PANI@CC-10 is 168 mAh g−1. When the current density is 10 A g−1, the specific discharge capacity is 102 mAh g−1, and the capacity retention rate is as high as 94.3 % after 10,000 cycles. Besides, the investigation on the energy storage mechanism shows that a part of Zn2+ is inserted to form NZnO bonds during the cycle, which facilitates faster and more storage of Zn2+. Therefore, CPDs/PANI@CC-10 can show better electrochemical performance and certain practical application potential. The proposed concept and the approach developed in this work provide a new possibility for the practical application of PANI in AZIBs.
